prs.title: Add auto-assign workflow for issue comments
prs.body:
## Description
Please include a summary of the changes and the related issue (if any). List any dependencies that are required for this change.

Fixes # (issue)

## Type of Change
Please delete options that are not relevant.
- [ ] Bug fix (non-breaking change which fixes an issue)
- [ ] New feature (non-breaking change which adds functionality)
- [ ] Breaking change (fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected)
- [ ] Documentation update

## How Has This Been Tested?
Please describe the tests that you ran to verify your changes. 
- [ ] Frontend linting and Vitest tests (`npm run lint`, `npm test`)
- [ ] Rust Clippy and tests (`cargo clippy`, `cargo test`)

## Checklist:
- [ ] My code follows the style guidelines of this project
- [ ] My commits follow conventional commits guidelines
- [ ] My changes generate no new warnings or compiler errors
- [ ] I have added tests that prove my fix is effective or that my feature works

issues.title: [Beginner] Add/verify ESLint + TypeScript lint script and document how to run it
issues.body:
Goal: Add a basic ESLint + TypeScript setup or verify existing lint config and add an npm script so contributors can run `npm run lint` easily.

What to do:
1) Check if an ESLint or TypeScript config already exists (.eslintrc.js/.eslintrc.json/tsconfig.json).
2) If missing, add a minimal configuration:
   - Install dev deps: `npm install -D eslint @typescript-eslint/parser @typescript-eslint/eslint-plugin` (or use pnpm/yarn).
   - Add `.eslintrc.json` with recommended settings for TypeScript.
3) Add a package.json script: `"lint": "eslint 'src/**/*.{ts,tsx,js,jsx}' --max-warnings=0"`.
4) Update CONTRIBUTING.md with the lint command and encourage running it before opening PRs.

Acceptance criteria:
- ESLint config added or verified to exist.
- `npm run lint` (or equivalent) works and is documented.

Labels: good first issue, documentation, help wanted

issues.title: [Beginner] Add an Architecture Overview doc (docs/ARCHITECTURE.md)
issues.body:
Goal: Add a short "Architecture Overview" doc that explains the high-level structure (Tauri core, Rust backend, TypeScript frontend, styling), so new contributors know where to make changes.

What to do:
1) Create a new file docs/ARCHITECTURE.md or update README with an "Architecture" section.
2) Include the following parts:
   - High-level diagram or ASCII flow showing how the frontend, Tauri, and Rust components interact.
   - Where the TypeScript frontend code lives and how UI components are organized.
   - Where Rust crates live (if multiple) and what responsibilities they have.
   - How hybrid local/cloud AI routing is structured at a high level (no secrets, just the flow).
   - Where to find configuration files, assets, and important build scripts.
3) Link to important files (e.g., top-level Cargo.toml, package.json, src directories) and mention common dev commands.

Acceptance criteria:
- docs/ARCHITECTURE.md (or README section) added with clear descriptions and links to code locations.
- The doc helps a reader answer: "Which file should I edit to change X?" for at least 5 common tasks.

Labels: good first issue, documentation

issues.title: [Beginner] Add a simple CI workflow: TypeScript lint and cargo check
issues.body:
Goal: Add a simple automated check in GitHub Actions that runs the TypeScript linter and a Rust `cargo check` so contributors get fast feedback.

What to do:
1) Add a workflow file .github/workflows/ci.yml with two jobs:
   - frontend-lint: checks out code, sets up Node, installs deps, runs `npm run lint` or `pnpm lint` (check package.json for the script name), and fails on warnings if configured.
   - rust-check: checks out code, sets up Rust toolchain (stable), runs `cargo check` in the Rust crate directories.
2) Keep the workflow simple and fast for first-time contributors.
3) Add comments in the workflow explaining how to add more steps (tests, build) later.

Acceptance criteria:
- A new GitHub Actions workflow file committed that runs on PRs and pushes to main/phase-1-scaffold.
- The workflow should be documented in the README or CONTRIBUTING.md so contributors know CI will run on their PR.

Labels: good first issue, help wanted

issues.title: [Beginner] Add a step-by-step "Run CNTRL locally" guide to README.md
issues.body:
Goal: Help a new developer get the project running locally so they can open the app, make changes, and submit a PR.

What to do:
1) Install prerequisites (examples):
   - Install Rust (stable, e.g., via https://rustup.rs). Verify with `rustc --version`.
   - Install Node.js (LTS, Node 18+ or 20). Verify with `node --version` and `npm --version`.
   - Install the Tauri toolchain: `cargo install tauri-cli` (or follow Tauri docs).
2) Clone the repo:
   - `git clone https://github.com/Demon-Die/CNTRL.git`
   - `cd CNTRL`
3) Install JavaScript dependencies:
   - Inspect the project root for package manager (package.json, pnpm-lock.yaml, yarn.lock).
   - If using npm: `npm install`
   - If using pnpm: `pnpm install`
   - If using yarn: `yarn install`
4) Start the development server / app:
   - Check package.json scripts. Common commands:
     - `npm run dev` or `pnpm dev` or `yarn dev` to start the webdev server
     - `npm run tauri dev` or `pnpm tauri dev` to run the Tauri app
   - If unsure, search package.json for scripts containing "dev" or "tauri".
5) Verify the app opens and the webview renders. Take a screenshot if anything fails.

How to document your work (required for the issue):
- Add a new section to README.md titled "Running the project locally" with the exact commands you used, any gotchas, and the environment you tested (OS, Node, Rust versions).
- If you had to run different commands than documented, update the README to reflect the correct workflow.

Acceptance criteria:
- A new README section that clearly explains how to run CNTRL locally on macOS and Linux/Windows (as applicable).
- A short comment on this issue describing the environment you tested on and confirming the commands work.

Labels: good first issue, documentation, help wanted

issues.title: [Beginner] Expand README: clear installation and prerequisites instructions
issues.body:
Goal: Improve the README installation / prerequisites section so new contributors don't get blocked by environment setup.

What to do:
1) Inspect README.md and the existing "Installation" or "Getting started" sections.
2) Identify missing details or ambiguous commands (example: missing `cargo`, missing Node version, Tauri setup).
3) Update the README with precise commands to install Rust (rustup), Node, and Tauri. Example commands to include:
   - `curl --proto '=https' --tlsv1.2 -sSf https://sh.rustup.rs | sh`
   - `curl -fsSL https://deb.nodesource.com/setup_20.x | sudo -E bash - && sudo apt-get install -y nodejs` (or point to Node installers/platform guides)
   - `cargo install tauri-cli`
4) Add troubleshooting tips for common errors (e.g., mismatched Node/Rust versions, missing `libgtk` on Linux for Tauri).

Acceptance criteria:
- README updated with clear, copy-paste commands and troubleshooting notes.
- If you verified commands on your environment, include OS and versions used.

Labels: good first issue, documentation

issues.title: [Beginner] Walk-through: make a tiny UI change and open a PR
issues.body:
Goal: Create a short "How to make a tiny UI change" walk-through that edits a CSS variable or small component so a new front-end contributor can make a visible change and open a PR.

What to do:
1) Identify a simple UI target such as a primary button color, logo alt text, or a header title.
2) Make a tiny change in the frontend (for example, update a CSS variable or change a string in a component). Keep the change to one file and one line if possible.
3) Run the dev server and take a before/after screenshot to include in the PR description.
4) Update docs/ or README with the step-by-step walk-through titled "Make a tiny UI change". The guide should include:
   - Files to edit (where frontend code lives)
   - Commands to run the dev server
   - How to create a branch, commit, and open a PR
   - How to include screenshots in the PR

Acceptance criteria:
- A short walk-through added to docs/ or README that demonstrates a tiny UI change.
- A sample PR or branch showing the change (optional but helpful).

Labels: good first issue, help wanted, documentation

issues.title: Make User Agent Configurable in Settings
issues.body:
### Description
Currently, the Chrome User Agent is hardcoded in `src-tauri/src/services/browser.rs`. We should allow advanced users to configure this in the settings interface.

### Requirements
- **Backend update**: Update `BrowserConfig` struct (or create one) to accept a custom `user_agent: Option<String>`.
- **Frontend Settings**: Add a "User Agent" text input field in `SettingsPage.tsx` under a new "Advanced Settings" section.
- **IPC command**: Modify Tauri commands to save the user agent and apply it to new tabs in `BrowserService`.

issues.title: Add Theme Selector Toggle (Dark/Light/System)
issues.body:
### Description
Allow users to switch between Light, Dark, or System default themes.

### Requirements
- **Settings UI**: Add a dropdown or toggle button in `SettingsPage.tsx` under a new "App Theme" section.
- **Storage**: Persist the selected theme in `localStorage` or via Tauri Store plugin.
- **CSS Themes**: Apply `data-theme="light"` or `data-theme="dark"` to the `<html>` or `<body>` element and define light/dark styling properties in `tokens.css`.

issues.title: Add Keyboard Shortcuts for Back, Forward, and Reload
issues.body:
### Description
Enhance keyboard navigation accessibility by listening for common browser shortcuts.

### Requirements
- **Shortcuts**:
  - `Cmd+R` / `Ctrl+R` to reload the active tab.
  - `Cmd+[` / `Alt+Left Arrow` to go back.
  - `Cmd+]` / `Alt+Right Arrow` to go forward.
- **Implementation**: Register event listeners in `App.tsx` or `UrlBar.tsx` and trigger the respective actions in `browserStore.ts`.

issues.title: Build Background Agent Runtime for Autonomous Scraping (Phase 6)
issues.body:
### Description
Implement a background worker runtime in Rust that allows running headless browser instances (or background WebView tabs) to autonomously browse, extract content, and navigate pages without blocking the user's view.

### Requirements
- **Headless Worker**: Allow spawning hidden Tauri webviews to execute script actions.
- **Communication**: Use Tauri events to notify the frontend when background agents have finished executing actions (e.g., finished summarizing a page).
- **Resource Management**: Limit active background workers to prevent system resource starvation (CPU/Memory).

issues.title: Securely Store AI API Keys using OS Keychain (Phase 5)
issues.body:
### Description
Currently, API keys like the OpenRouter key are written as plain text files in the app data directory. We need to integrate OS Keychain services (Keychain on macOS, Credential Manager on Windows, Secret Service on Linux) to store sensitive credentials securely.

### Requirements
- **Library**: Use the Rust `keyring` crate.
- **Integration**:
  - Modify `src-tauri/src/services/ai_router.rs` where the OpenRouter key is read/written.
  - Instead of `fs::write(&self.storage_path, key)`, store it in the OS secure credential store under the target `com.cntrl.browser.openrouter`.
- **Error Handling**: Gracefully fallback to files/in-memory if the keychain is locked or unavailable.

issues.title: Integrate SQLite Database for Session and History Persistence (Phase 5)
issues.body:
### Description
Replace temporary in-memory state with a persistent SQLite database to save user history, active tabs, bookmarks, and configuration parameters across app restarts.

### Requirements
- **Database setup**: Use `rusqlite` or `sqlx` in the Tauri Rust backend.
- **Data Models**:
  - `history`: `id`, `url`, `title`, `visited_at`
  - `bookmarks`: `id`, `url`, `title`, `created_at`
  - `tabs_session`: Save current open tabs and which one is active so we can restore the user's previous session on start.
- **Rust Migrations**: Write standard setup script to initialize schemas on app startup.

issues.title: Develop Natural Language Intent Classifier & Routing Logic (Phase 4)
issues.body:
### Description
Build the intent parsing engine that takes natural language input from the command bar, classifies the action, and routes it to the appropriate browser execution handler.

### Requirements
- **LLM Prompting**: Design structured system prompts for local (Ollama) and cloud (OpenRouter) models to reliably output JSON schemas representing actions.
- **Intent Types to Support**:
  - `navigate` (e.g., "go to news.ycombinator.com")
  - `search` (e.g., "search for latest Rust web frameworks")
  - `action` (e.g., "scroll down", "click log in button", "screenshot this page")
- **Error Handling**: Handle fallback states gracefully when the LLM response is malformed or classification confidence is low.
- **Rust Integration**: Implement this in `src-tauri/src/services/ai_router.rs` or a new `intent_parser.rs` service.

issues.title: Implement Raycast-style Command Bar UI (Phase 4)
issues.body:
### Description
Implement a central command bar (omnibar) interface similar to Spotlight, Raycast, or Alfred. This bar will be the primary entry point for users to input natural language browser intents.

### Requirements
- **Hotkey Trigger**: Pressing `Cmd+K` (macOS) or `Ctrl+K` (Windows/Linux) should toggle the Command Bar overlay.
- **Fuzzy Search & Autocomplete**: As the user types, show history recommendations, search suggestions, or matching local commands.
- **UI/UX Design**:
  - Central modal with smooth entry animation.
  - Backdrop filter blur effect (glassmorphism) over the main web view.
  - Keyboard navigation (Arrow keys to navigate results, `Enter` to select, `Esc` to close).
- **State Integration**: Should dispatch typed commands/intents to the intent router backend service.

issues.title: chore(dev): make first-run local validation reproducible
issues.body:
### Problem

Running `pnpm lint` in a fresh checkout fails before linting because dependencies are not installed locally:

```text
sh: turbo: command not found
Local package.json exists, but node_modules missing, did you mean to install?
```

That is expected before `pnpm install`, but the repo also lacks a single documented "verify my setup" command and does not currently include migrations/seed scripts referenced by README.

### Suggested fix

Add a clear first-run validation path:

- document `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`
- add or document `pnpm build`, `pnpm lint`, and service-specific checks
- either add the referenced Prisma migrations/seed files or mark them planned
- consider a root `check` script once tests exist

### Acceptance criteria

- A contributor can clone, install, and run one documented validation sequence.
- Setup docs do not reference missing seed/migration files as if they exist.
- The root scripts map cleanly to workspace scripts.

issues.title: chore: add GitHub issue templates, PR template, and contributor labels
issues.body:
### Problem

The project asks contributors to use GitHub issues before contributing, but there are no issue templates or PR template yet. As the repo opens up, maintainers will get more consistent contributions if GitHub guides the shape of reports and PRs.

### Suggested fix

Add:

- `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/bug_report.yml`
- `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/feature_request.yml`
- `.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/docs.yml`
- `.github/pull_request_template.md`
- starter labels such as `area:web`, `area:api`, `area:ai-service`, `area:docs`, `priority:p0`, `priority:p1`, `priority:p2`, `good first issue`, and `help wanted`

### Acceptance criteria

- New issues ask for expected/actual behavior, setup context, and affected area.
- New PRs ask for summary, screenshots if UI, test evidence, and linked issue.
- Area and priority labels exist or are documented for maintainers to create.

---

issues.title: security: add SECURITY.md and tighten public-facing defaults
issues.body:
### Problem

The repo is public and AI/auth-enabled, but it does not yet have a `SECURITY.md`. A few current defaults also need tightening before contributors build on them:

- Code of Conduct report contact is still `your-email@example.com` (`CODE_OF_CONDUCT.md` line 33).
- Express and Socket.io use unrestricted CORS (`apps/api/src/index.ts` lines 71-85).
- FastAPI uses unrestricted CORS with credentials enabled (`apps/ai-service/app/main.py` lines 12-18).
- `.env.example` includes realistic-looking key prefixes, which can confuse secret scanners and contributors.

### Suggested fix

- Add `SECURITY.md` with supported versions and vulnerability reporting instructions.
- Replace placeholder conduct email with a real maintainer contact or GitHub security advisory path.
- Move CORS origins to environment variables and default to localhost in development.
- Change example secrets to obviously fake placeholders.

### Acceptance criteria

- `SECURITY.md` exists.
- Code of Conduct has a real reporting path.
- API and AI service CORS origins are configurable.
- Example secrets cannot be mistaken for real credentials.

---

issues.title: feat(ai-service): replace mock AI/diff/quiz/defend routes with real service boundaries
issues.body:
The AI service endpoints are currently mock implementations:

- `generate.py` checks `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` but still returns mock text even when a key exists (`apps/ai-service/app/routes/generate.py` lines 18-28).
- quiz generation returns sample questions from query params rather than annotated code.
- defend evaluation passes after message count rather than evaluating the answer.
- diff returns a fixed sample diff instead of comparing the submitted code.

These are fine for scaffolding, but they do not yet implement the README's core Decode/Rebuild/Defend learning loop.

### Suggested fix

Introduce clear service modules and request/response contracts:

- Claude client wrapper with timeout/error handling
- quiz generation from code/annotations
- defend question/evaluation flow
- actual diff/scoring engine
- tests for each route with mocked external providers

### Acceptance criteria

- Routes no longer return fixed sample content in normal execution.
- Missing provider keys fail clearly or enter an explicit local mock mode.
- External AI calls are isolated behind service classes/functions.
- Each route has at least one test for success and one for error behavior.

---

issues.title: feat(ai-service): replace mock AI/diff/quiz/defend routes with real service boundaries
issues.body:
Problem
apps/web/src/middleware.ts exports NextAuth middleware for nearly every route except API/static/image/favicon (apps/web/src/middleware.ts lines 1-5). The auth config points sign-in to /auth/signin, but that route is not present in the current app.

This can create a broken first-run experience where protected routing sends users to a missing sign-in page, or future public pages become protected unintentionally.

### Suggested fix

Define an explicit route access policy:

- keep `/`, `/auth/signin`, and other public pages public
- protect dashboard/module/private app routes
- add the actual sign-in page or remove the custom sign-in override until it exists

### Acceptance criteria

- Public routes remain accessible without a session.
- Protected routes redirect to a real sign-in page.
- Missing `/auth/signin` behavior is resolved.
- Route matcher includes a short comment explaining the policy.

---

issues.title: feat(web): replace the default Next.js starter page with an UnVibe MVP shell
issues.body:
The web app still renders the default Create Next App page. It shows Next.js/Vercel links and starter instructions (`apps/web/src/app/page.tsx` lines 1-101), so the first user-facing screen does not match the UnVibe product described in the README.

Replace the starter page with a minimal UnVibe application shell:

- brand/product header
- short product positioning
- entry points for Decode, Rebuild, Defend, Dashboard, and Tracks
- placeholder states where full features are not implemented yet

### Acceptance criteria

- No Next.js/Vercel starter content remains on `/`.
- `/` clearly presents UnVibe.
- The page does not imply unavailable functionality is production-ready.
- The page works responsively on mobile and desktop.

---

issues.title: chore: add CI workflow for lint, typecheck, build, and basic service validation
issues.body:
The README says .github/workflows/ci.yml exists and runs checks on PRs, but the repo currently has no .github/ workflow files. That means contributors can open PRs without automated verification.

Add a GitHub Actions workflow that runs on pull requests and pushes to `main`.

Recommended first pass:

- install pnpm using the version from `package.json`
- run `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`
- run `pnpm lint`
- run `pnpm build`
- add Python setup for `apps/ai-service`
- run a minimal FastAPI import or health-test command

### Acceptance criteria

- `.github/workflows/ci.yml` exists.
- CI runs for PRs and pushes to `main`.
- CI fails on TypeScript/build/lint errors.
- CI verifies the Python service can import/start at least minimally.

---

issues.title: docs: reconcile README with the actual scaffold and setup commands
issues.body:
The README describes a much larger repo than currently exists. That makes the project look harder to contribute to and sends new contributors looking for files that are not present yet.

Examples:

- `README.md` says the repo contains route groups, shared UI, backend routers/services, AI prompt folders, infra scripts, and GitHub workflows, but many of those paths are not in the current tree (`README.md` lines 56-142).
- The clone command still uses `https://github.com/your-org/unvibe.git` instead of this repo (`README.md` lines 173-178).
- README says the API runs on `3001`, while `apps/api/src/index.ts` defaults to `4000` and `CONTRIBUTING.md` documents `4000`.
- README says pnpm `9` is required (`README.md` line 154), while `package.json` pins `pnpm@10.18.0`.

### Suggested fix

Update README to clearly distinguish:

- what exists today
- what is planned
- the current local setup path
- the current service ports

### Acceptance criteria

- README clone URL points to `Demon-Die/UnVibe`.
- Project tree reflects the current scaffold, or planned directories are marked as planned.
- Port references are consistent across README, CONTRIBUTING, and package scripts.
- pnpm version guidance matches `packageManager`.

---

issues.title: The Get Started Page is not Working .
issues.body: I want to make a Dashboard Based on the websites theme , where I will make navigation bar , header , footers and add some important components before you can start putting on content.
